content:
# Luke Chapter 7 — The PNEUMA Bible (Dynamic)
**Translator:** PNEUMA | **Source:** Codex Sinaiticus, PDF pages 93-95

---

**1** When Jesus had finished saying all this to the people who were listening, he entered Capernaum. **2** There a centurion had a slave who was dear to him, and the slave was sick and close to death. **3** When the centurion heard about Jesus, he sent some Jewish elders to him, asking him to come and save his slave's life. **4** They came to Jesus and pleaded earnestly with him. "This man deserves your help," they said. **5** "He loves our nation, and he is the one who built our synagogue."

**6** So Jesus went with them. But when he was not far from the house, the centurion sent friends with a message: "Lord, do not trouble yourself. I am not worthy to have you come under my roof. **7** That is why I did not even consider myself worthy to come to you in person. But just say the word, and let my servant be healed. **8** For I myself am a man placed under authority, with soldiers under me. I tell one, 'Go,' and he goes. I tell another, 'Come,' and he comes. I tell my slave, 'Do this,' and he does it."

**9** When Jesus heard this, he was amazed at him. He turned to the crowd following him and said, "I tell you, not even in Israel have I found faith like this." **10** And when the messengers returned to the house, they found the slave in good health.

**11** Soon afterward, he went to a town called Nain. His disciples and a large crowd traveled with him. **12** As he approached the town gate, a dead man was being carried out — the only son of his mother, and she was a widow. A considerable crowd from the town was with her.

**13** When the Lord saw her, his heart broke for her, and he said to her, "Do not weep." **14** Then he went up and touched the bier, and the pallbearers stood still. He said, "Young man, I say to you, get up!" **15** The dead man sat up and began to speak, and Jesus gave him back to his mother.

**16** Awe gripped them all, and they glorified God, saying, "A great prophet has risen among us!" and "God has come to the aid of his people!" **17** This report about him spread throughout all of Judea and the entire surrounding region.

**18** John's disciples brought word of all these things to him. **19** So John summoned two of his disciples and sent them to the Lord to ask, "Are you the one who is coming, or should we expect someone else?"

**20** When the men reached Jesus, they said, "John the Baptist sent us to you to ask: 'Are you the one who is coming, or should we expect someone else?'"

**21** At that very hour, Jesus healed many people of diseases, afflictions, and evil spirits, and granted sight to many who were blind. **22** Then he answered them, "Go back and tell John what you have seen and heard: the blind see, the lame walk, those with skin diseases are cleansed, the deaf hear, the dead are raised, and the poor are hearing good news. **23** And blessed is the person who does not stumble over me."

**24** After John's messengers had left, Jesus began to speak to the crowds about John: "What did you go out into the wilderness to see? A reed swaying in the wind? **25** Then what did you go out to see? A man dressed in fine clothing? Look — those who wear splendid clothes and live in luxury are found in royal palaces. **26** So what did you go out to see? A prophet? Yes, I tell you, and far more than a prophet. **27** This is the one about whom it is written:

'Look, I am sending my messenger ahead of you,
who will prepare your way before you.'

**28** "I tell you, among those born of women, no one is greater than John. Yet the least person in the kingdom of God is greater than he."

**29** All the people who heard this — even the tax collectors — affirmed God's justice, having been baptized with John's baptism. **30** But the Pharisees and the experts in the law rejected God's purpose for themselves, since they had not been baptized by him.

**31** "So what comparison fits the people of this generation? What are they like? **32** They are like children sitting in the marketplace and calling out to one another:

'We played the flute for you, and you did not dance.
We sang a funeral song, and you did not weep.'

**33** "For John the Baptist came neither eating bread nor drinking wine, and you say, 'He has a demon.' **34** The Son of Man came eating and drinking, and you say, 'Look at this man — a glutton and a drunkard, a friend of tax collectors and sinners!' **35** Yet wisdom is vindicated by all her children."

**36** One of the Pharisees invited Jesus to eat with him. So he went to the Pharisee's house and reclined at the table. **37** A woman from the town who had lived a sinful life learned that Jesus was dining at the Pharisee's house. She brought an alabaster jar of perfumed oil **38** and stood behind him at his feet, weeping. She began to wet his feet with her tears, and she wiped them with her hair, kissing his feet and anointing them with the perfumed oil.

**39** When the Pharisee who had invited him saw this, he said to himself, "If this man were really a prophet, he would know who this woman is — what kind of person is touching him. She is a sinner."

**40** Jesus responded, "Simon, I have something to say to you."

"Say it, Teacher," he replied.

**41** "Two people owed money to a certain lender. One owed five hundred silver coins, the other fifty. **42** Neither of them could pay it back, so he graciously forgave them both. Now, which of them will love him more?"

**43** Simon answered, "I suppose the one who had the larger debt forgiven."

"You have judged correctly," Jesus said.

**44** Then he turned toward the woman and said to Simon, "Do you see this woman? I came into your house. You gave me no water for my feet, but she has drenched my feet with her tears and dried them with her hair. **45** You gave me no kiss of greeting, but from the moment I arrived she has not stopped kissing my feet. **46** You did not anoint my head with oil, but she has anointed my feet with perfumed oil.

**47** "This is why I tell you: her many sins have been forgiven — and her great love proves it. But the one who is forgiven little loves little."

**48** Then he said to her, "Your sins are forgiven."

**49** The other guests began to say among themselves, "Who is this, who even forgives sins?"

**50** But Jesus said to the woman, "Your faith has saved you. Go in peace."

---

## PNEUMA's Meaning Notes

1. **7:2 — "Dear to him" (entimos)**: The Sinaiticus reads *entimos* — "honored, precious, valued." This is not a casual relationship. The centurion's deep personal attachment to his slave was culturally unusual and speaks to a man of uncommon character. This explains the extraordinary effort he makes on the slave's behalf.

2. **7:3 — Elders as intermediaries**: In Luke's account (differing from Matthew's more compressed version), the centurion never approaches Jesus directly. He sends Jewish elders first, then friends. This layered approach reveals both cultural sensitivity — a Gentile soldier using appropriate channels — and a profound humility that Jesus will soon celebrate.

3. **7:6-8 — "Just say the word"**: The centurion's logic is brilliantly simple: he understands chains of command. As a military man, he knows that authority does not require physical presence. If his own spoken orders are carried out at a distance, how much more the word of Jesus? His faith is rooted not in mystical feeling but in a clear-eyed recognition of who Jesus is.

4. **7:9 — "Not even in Israel"**: Jesus' astonishment is striking — the Gospels rarely describe him as amazed. That this faith surpasses anything he has encountered in Israel underscores one of Luke's central themes: outsiders often grasp what insiders miss. A Roman officer understands Jesus' authority more clearly than the religious establishment.

5. **7:11-12 — The widow of Nain**: Luke constructs this scene with devastating economy. "Only son" (*monogenes*), "his mother," "widow." Each detail compounds the tragedy. Without husband or son, this woman faced not only grief but social and economic ruin. No one asks Jesus to act here — he sees, he feels, he moves.

6. **7:13 — "His heart broke for her" (esplagchnisthe)**: The Greek *splagchnizomai* refers to a visceral, gut-level compassion — the churning of the internal organs. This is the deepest word for compassion in the Greek language. It is used almost exclusively of Jesus or of God-figures in the parables. "His heart broke for her" captures the emotional intensity better than the clinical "he had compassion."

7. **7:14 — Touching the bier**: By touching the funeral bier (*soros*), Jesus would have contracted ritual impurity under Torah law. But in Luke's theology, purity flows outward from Jesus rather than impurity flowing inward. The unclean does not contaminate him; he decontaminates what he touches. The dead man rises; Jesus remains clean.

8. **7:16 — "God has come to the aid of his people"**: The Greek *epeskepsato* ("visited, come to the aid of") echoes Zechariah's prophecy in Luke 1:68 and 1:78 — God "visiting" his people. The crowd at Nain, without realizing the full weight of their words, is speaking a deeper truth than they know: God is indeed present among them in person.

9. **7:22 — The messianic catalogue**: Jesus' answer to John draws from Isaiah 29:18-19, 35:5-6, and 61:1. He does not say "Yes, I am the Messiah." Instead, he points to the evidence and lets John draw the conclusion. Each item — blind seeing, lame walking, lepers cleansed, deaf hearing, dead raised, poor hearing good news — corresponds to prophetic expectations about the age of salvation.

10. **7:23 — "Stumble over me" (skandalisthe)**: The word *skandalizo* means to be tripped up, caused to fall, offended. This is a gentle but pointed word directed at John himself, who from prison may have expected a different kind of Messiah — one who would overthrow Rome and bring immediate justice. Jesus acknowledges the difficulty: his messiahship does not look like what anyone expected.

11. **7:27 — Malachi 3:1**: The quotation combines elements of Exodus 23:20 and Malachi 3:1. By applying this to John, Jesus identifies him as the forerunner who prepares the way for God's own arrival. The implication is breathtaking: if John is the messenger sent before "the Lord," then Jesus is the Lord whose coming was foretold.

12. **7:28 — "The least in the kingdom"**: This is one of the most startling statements in the Gospels. John is the greatest figure of the old era, yet the new reality Jesus inaugurates is of a qualitatively different order. Even the most ordinary participant in the kingdom of God inhabits a reality that transcends anything the old covenant could offer. This is not a diminishment of John but an exaltation of the kingdom.

13. **7:29-30 — Affirming and rejecting God's purpose**: The Sinaiticus text presents these verses as narration rather than as words of Jesus. The tax collectors — society's outcasts — "justified God" (*edikaiosan ton theon*), meaning they affirmed God's rightness by accepting John's baptism. The Pharisees and legal experts, by refusing baptism, "rejected God's purpose for themselves" (*ten boulen tou theou ethetesen*). The irony is devastating: the "sinners" aligned with God's plan; the "righteous" blocked it.

14. **7:32 — Children in the marketplace**: The parable captures a petulant refusal to engage. The children of "this generation" reject both John's asceticism (the funeral song) and Jesus' celebration (the flute music). No approach satisfies them. Their objection is not rational but temperamental — they have decided in advance to reject whatever God sends.

15. **7:35 — "Wisdom is vindicated by all her children"**: Sinaiticus reads *panton ton teknon autes* — "all her children." Wisdom (personified as female, following Jewish tradition) is proven right not by argument but by the lives of those she produces. John's disciples and Jesus' followers — different as they are — both bear fruit that validates divine wisdom's strategy.

16. **7:37-38 — The woman's extravagant act**: Every detail is charged with cultural transgression. A woman of the city (*hamartolos* — "sinner," likely a euphemism for a prostitute) enters a Pharisee's house uninvited. She lets down her hair in public (a scandalous act for a Jewish woman). She touches a rabbi. She weeps on his feet. She uses expensive perfumed oil (*myron*). The scene is simultaneously shocking and deeply moving — an eruption of gratitude that shatters every social boundary.

17. **7:41-43 — The parable of the two debtors**: Five hundred denarii represented roughly two years' wages for a day laborer; fifty denarii was about two months'. The parable is elegant in its simplicity: the greater the forgiveness, the greater the love. But Jesus is not saying the woman earned forgiveness through love. The logic runs the other direction — she loves much *because* she has been forgiven much. Forgiveness is the cause; love is the evidence.

18. **7:44-46 — Simon's three failures**: Jesus draws a devastating contrast. Simon, the proper host, failed in three basic duties of hospitality: water for feet, a kiss of greeting, oil for the head. The sinful woman exceeded all three — with tears instead of water, with unceasing kisses, with costly perfumed oil on his feet rather than common oil on his head. The one who owed nothing to social convention outperformed the one who knew every rule.

19. **7:47 — "Her great love proves it"**: This verse is often misread. The Greek *hoti egapesen poly* ("because she loved much") explains not why her sins were forgiven but how we can tell they have been. The parable in vv. 41-43 makes the sequence clear: forgiveness comes first, then love follows as its fruit. The woman's lavish devotion is the visible proof of an invisible grace already received.

20. **7:50 — "Your faith has saved you"**: The Greek *sesoken* can mean both "saved" and "healed." Jesus attributes her salvation not to her tears, her ointment, or her love, but to her *faith*. This is the theological capstone of the entire episode: faith is the channel through which forgiveness flows. "Go in peace" (*poreuou eis eirenen*) — the Hebraic *shalom* — signals a wholeness that encompasses far more than the absence of conflict. She leaves restored, reconciled, whole.
